text: Francesco Cirillo invented the Pomodoro Technique in the late 1980s as a university student, naming it after the tomato-shaped kitchen timer he used to track work intervals. The method is deceptively simple: work for twenty-five minutes with full concentration, then take a five-minute break. What Cirillo discovered — and what neuroscience later confirmed — is that the brain resists open-ended effort but accepts bounded challenges. The ticking timer transforms an amorphous task into a finite contest. Urgency is manufactured on demand. Four pomodoros constitute one session, after which a longer rest of fifteen to thirty minutes allows the hippocampus to consolidate what was processed.
question: According to the passage, what psychological shift does the timer create that makes focused work easier?

options.text: It transforms an open-ended task into a finite, bounded challenge
It eliminates the urge to check notifications during work
It trains the hippocampus to form memories more quickly
It replicates the social urgency of a deadline set by others
